summary: Specify sort order without prefixing field name with "-"
description:
It's possible for an index to be created on a field that contains a "-" character, so the query sort function in mgo
{{func (*Query) Sort(fields ...string)}} could receive an arg like ("-field1") and not be able to distinguish a descending sort on "field1", from an ascending sort on "-field1".

summary: Support PLAIN auth without external SASL library
description: The SASL library incurs in additional runtime dependencies. Implement and test the PLAIN mechanism without it.

summary: Potential deadlocking and leaking of DNS goroutines
description:
It appears that the deadline support for DNS lookups in MGO-34 may have had a couple unintended side-effects:

1.) By introducing the deadline support for DNS resolution, the mgo driver can give up / timeout an unresponsive DNS request, allowing forward progress to be made - a good thing. However given that the same DNS code path in the go standard library is still being used to perform the lookup, this spawned "worker" goroutine can still sometimes hang indefinitely in the C function call for {{getaddrinfo}}. As a result, it's been observed that these permanently stalled DNS goroutines can accumulate introducing a kind of goroutine "leak".

2.) Additionally, when the first goroutine happens to stall on the C function {{getaddrinfo}}, it appears to also prevent an important global condition variable from ever being signaled again within go's DNS implementation. This _appears_ to in turn cause future DNS attempts (perhaps _all_ future attempts, but I'm less clear on this) to timeout and give up, causing a much faster than expected accumulation of these zombie DNS goroutines. 

So far we've seen stack dumps of two occurrences of this phenomena, on two distinct completely unrelated networks.
* The 1st trace contained 51,879 goroutines that are awaiting this {{WaitGroup}} signal:

{noformat}
goroutine 338796 [semacquire, 8180 minutes]:
sync.runtime_Semacquire(0xc208835e70)
        /usr/local/go/src/pkg/runtime/sema.goc:199 +0x30
sync.(*WaitGroup).Wait(0xc208cb7840)
        /usr/local/go/src/pkg/sync/waitgroup.go:129 +0x14b
net.(*singleflight).Do(0xc8e560, 0xc2082569e0, 0x7, 0x7f2dc8a8eea0, 0x0, 0x0, 0x0, 0x0, 0x0)
        /usr/local/go/src/pkg/net/singleflight.go:37 +0x127
net.lookupIPMerge(0xc2082569e0, 0x7, 0x0, 0x0, 0x0, 0x0, 0x0)
        /usr/local/go/src/pkg/net/lookup.go:42 +0xae
net.func·025()
        /usr/local/go/src/pkg/net/lookup.go:80 +0x3e
created by net.lookupIPDeadline
        /usr/local/go/src/pkg/net/lookup.go:82 +0x2d8
{noformat}

...while 2 permanently hung goroutines are stuck in {{getaddrinfo}}, preventing the signal from occurring:

{noformat}
goroutine 336290 [syscall, 8248 minutes]:
net._C2func_getaddrinfo(0x7f2db00008c0, 0x0, 0xc208c6f800, 0xc2082ca0f0, 0x4fecd9, 0xc20829e258, 0xc208c74000)
        net/_obj/_cgo_defun.c:52 +0x36
net.cgoLookupIPCNAME(0xc208b61410, 0x7, 0x0, 0x0, 0x0, 0x0, 0x0, 0x0, 0x0, 0x0)
        /usr/local/go/src/pkg/net/cgo_unix.go:96 +0x1e1
net.cgoLookupIP(0xc208b61410, 0x7, 0x0, 0x0, 0x0, 0x0, 0x0, 0xc208027350)
        /usr/local/go/src/pkg/net/cgo_unix.go:148 +0x69
net.lookupIP(0xc208b61410, 0x7, 0x0, 0x0, 0x0, 0x0, 0x0)
        /usr/local/go/src/pkg/net/lookup_unix.go:64 +0x66
net.func·024(0x0, 0x0, 0x0, 0x0)
        /usr/local/go/src/pkg/net/lookup.go:41 +0x54
net.(*singleflight).Do(0xc8e560, 0xc208b61410, 0x7, 0x7f2dc8117ea0, 0x0, 0x0, 0x0, 0x0, 0xc201042ac0)
        /usr/local/go/src/pkg/net/singleflight.go:45 +0x232
net.lookupIPMerge(0xc208b61410, 0x7, 0x0, 0x0, 0x0, 0x0, 0x0)
        /usr/local/go/src/pkg/net/lookup.go:42 +0xae
net.func·025()
        /usr/local/go/src/pkg/net/lookup.go:80 +0x3e
created by net.lookupIPDeadline
        /usr/local/go/src/pkg/net/lookup.go:82 +0x2d8
{noformat}

* The 2nd trace contained ~21k abandoned goroutines awaiting the signal, and 1 goroutine stalled in {{getaddrinfo}}.

It seems like instead of timing out DNS requests using the deadline, we might want to explore using another DNS library if it gets us away from this {{_C2func_getaddrinfo}} entirely. One popular-ish options seems to be https://github.com/miekg/dns but it's unclear if:

a.) it's fully cross-platform including Windows and the variety of filesystem config files that can affect DNS.
b.) it's implementation doesn't itself fall back to go's C function usage.

Please let me know if I can provide any further details - thank you!

summary: Implement the SCRAM-SHA-1 SASL Mechanism
description:
Server 2.8 will be implementing SCRAM-SHA-1 as a new authentication mechanism. Eventually, it will become the default mechanism and MONGODB-CR will be deprecated and removed.

As such, mgo will need to support SCRAM-SHA-1 for the 2.8 release. RFC 5802 describes the mechanism and how it should be implemented.

summary: Serverside cursors that time out cannot be distinguished from exhausted cursors.
description:
Attached is a script that does the following:
# creates 100 documents
# does a collection scan
# iterates over one document
# sleeps for 11 minutes (takes 10 minutes to cause a cursor timeout)
# demonstrates the following `getMore` does not return any documents and there is no error on the iterator.

summary: PoolLimit to DialInfo does not behave as expected
description:
It appears the {{PoolLimit}} value specified on a DialInfo is not considered by either the initial cluster sync or pinger. Further, the check for exceeding the limit appears to race with other "in-flight" connection attempts.

For example, say I dial with a PoolLimit of 3, and then start goroutines that each get a {{Copy()}} of the session and perform operations. Each of these goroutines will then race on hitting the pool limit, and very likely end up continuing on to establish a connection, resulting in more than 3 sockets being created.

That said, I can understand why the internal cluster sync and pinger routines don't have the limit applied, as well as why these "in-flight" connection attempts aren't tracked against the limit (although it seems like an option). However what did surprise me is that "excess" sockets are not closed upon being recycled back to the pool when we can see that we already have {{PoolLimit}} sockets. 

What this behavior means for my {{PoolLimit:3}} example above is my application may end up creating 20 connections at initial startup, yet from then on only 3 of these connections will ever be considered again, while the other 17 will remain alive but permanently "idle" since they won't ever be allocated from the pool.

Simple reproducer program attached that matches this pattern and periodically prints socket stats. (Separately, I noticed {{SlaveConns}} can become negative at times, which seems odd.)

summary: Apparent deadlock between closing Iter and cluster sync
description:
Below are the relevant traces to an apparent deadlock between a user calling {{Iter.Close()}} and a cluster's {{syncServersIteration()}}.

*Application calling Iter.Close()*
{noformat}
goroutine 629 [semacquire, 31296 minutes]:
sync.runtime_Syncsemacquire(0xc209a87018)
        /usr/local/go/src/pkg/runtime/sema.goc:257 +0xc0
sync.(*Cond).Wait(0xc209a87008)
        /usr/local/go/src/pkg/sync/cond.go:62 +0x9d
gopkg.in/mgo%2ev2.(*mongoCluster).AcquireSocket(0xc209a86ff0, 0xc209a86f01, 0x9502f9000, 0x9502f9000, 0x0, 0x0, 0x0, 0x1000, 0x2, 0x0, ...)
        /mnt/jenkins/workspace/Monitoring-Agent-Debian/build/build/go-dependencies/src/gopkg.in/mgo.v2/cluster.go:548 +0x7af
gopkg.in/mgo%2ev2.(*Session).acquireSocket(0xc2099cb680, 0x1, 0x0, 0x0, 0x0)
        /mnt/jenkins/workspace/Monitoring-Agent-Debian/build/build/go-dependencies/src/gopkg.in/mgo.v2/session.go:3641 +0x2f6
gopkg.in/mgo%2ev2.(*Iter).acquireSocket(0xc210a142d0, 0xc2106b18c0, 0x0, 0x0)
        /mnt/jenkins/workspace/Monitoring-Agent-Debian/build/build/go-dependencies/src/gopkg.in/mgo.v2/session.go:3110 +0x48
gopkg.in/mgo%2ev2.(*Iter).killCursor(0xc210a142d0, 0x0, 0x0)
        /mnt/jenkins/workspace/Monitoring-Agent-Debian/build/build/go-dependencies/src/gopkg.in/mgo.v2/session.go:2892 +0x5b
gopkg.in/mgo%2ev2.(*Iter).Close(0xc210a142d0, 0x0, 0x0)
        /mnt/jenkins/workspace/Monitoring-Agent-Debian/build/build/go-dependencies/src/gopkg.in/mgo.v2/session.go:2881 +0x52
mongodb.com/monitoring-agent/components.func·030(0x489e01)
        /mnt/jenkins/workspace/Monitoring-Agent-Debian/build/build/go-dependencies/src/mongodb.com/monitoring-agent/components/task.go:922 +0x68d
mongodb.com/monitoring-agent/components.(*CustomRunnerTask).Run(0xc20b203e80, 0x259447f6)
{noformat}

*syncServersIteration() traces:*
{noformat}
goroutine 1057 [semacquire, 31297 minutes]:
sync.runtime_Semacquire(0xc20a1b6a24)
        /usr/local/go/src/pkg/runtime/sema.goc:199 +0x30
sync.(*WaitGroup).Wait(0xc20a3e9a40)
        /usr/local/go/src/pkg/sync/waitgroup.go:129 +0x14b
gopkg.in/mgo%2ev2.(*mongoCluster).syncServersIteration(0xc209a86ff0, 0x1)
        /mnt/jenkins/workspace/Monitoring-Agent-Debian/build/build/go-dependencies/src/gopkg.in/mgo.v2/cluster.go:489 +0x3b5
gopkg.in/mgo%2ev2.(*mongoCluster).syncServersLoop(0xc209a86ff0)
        /mnt/jenkins/workspace/Monitoring-Agent-Debian/build/build/go-dependencies/src/gopkg.in/mgo.v2/cluster.go:342 +0x1e1
created by gopkg.in/mgo%2ev2.newCluster
        /mnt/jenkins/workspace/Monitoring-Agent-Debian/build/build/go-dependencies/src/gopkg.in/mgo.v2/cluster.go:73 +0x153

goroutine 1940595 [semacquire, 31296 minutes]:
sync.runtime_Semacquire(0xc210a142d4)
        /usr/local/go/src/pkg/runtime/sema.goc:199 +0x30
sync.(*Mutex).Lock(0xc210a142d0)
        /usr/local/go/src/pkg/sync/mutex.go:66 +0xd6
gopkg.in/mgo%2ev2.func·014(0x7f3c310a61b0, 0xc20b7aae30, 0x0, 0xffffffffffffffff, 0x0, 0x0, 0x0)
        /mnt/jenkins/workspace/Monitoring-Agent-Debian/build/build/go-dependencies/src/gopkg.in/mgo.v2/session.go:3699 +0x49
gopkg.in/mgo%2ev2.(*mongoSocket).kill(0xc20e1d21c0, 0x7f3c310a61b0, 0xc20b7aae30, 0xc20dc19b00)
        /mnt/jenkins/workspace/Monitoring-Agent-Debian/build/build/go-dependencies/src/gopkg.in/mgo.v2/socket.go:310 +0x6bb
gopkg.in/mgo%2ev2.(*mongoSocket).Close(0xc20e1d21c0)
        /mnt/jenkins/workspace/Monitoring-Agent-Debian/build/build/go-dependencies/src/gopkg.in/mgo.v2/socket.go:288 +0xb2
gopkg.in/mgo%2ev2.(*mongoServer).Close(0xc20dc19b20)
        /mnt/jenkins/workspace/Monitoring-Agent-Debian/build/build/go-dependencies/src/gopkg.in/mgo.v2/server.go:194 +0x20f
gopkg.in/mgo%2ev2.(*mongoCluster).removeServer(0xc209a86ff0, 0xc20dc19b20)
        /mnt/jenkins/workspace/Monitoring-Agent-Debian/build/build/go-dependencies/src/gopkg.in/mgo.v2/cluster.go:120 +0xdf
gopkg.in/mgo%2ev2.func·009()
        /mnt/jenkins/workspace/Monitoring-Agent-Debian/build/build/go-dependencies/src/gopkg.in/mgo.v2/cluster.go:462 +0x60d
created by gopkg.in/mgo%2ev2.func·010
        /mnt/jenkins/workspace/Monitoring-Agent-Debian/build/build/go-dependencies/src/gopkg.in/mgo.v2/cluster.go:482 +0x14c
{noformat}

Followed the code traces, what I think's the rough sequence (assumes these are occurring in parallel - not implying any ordering of the operations between 1 and 2):

1.) Application performs an Iter.Close()
	* acquires iter.m.Lock()
	* has to acquire socket from cluster
	* no existing sockets suitable, so triggers cluster sync and waits for it to complete.

2.) mongoCluster.syncServersIteration()
	* cluster.syncServer(server) encounters an error, so tries to remove server.
	* when calling replyFuncs on sockets, gets the replyFunc for our iter.Close(), which thens attempts to acquire iter.m.Lock() which is already held.

While this trace is now ~5 commits behind current tip of v2 branch, my surface-level look suggests the underlying issue's still present.

summary: MotorCursor to_list ignores _fix_outgoing
description:
{{MotorCursor().to_list}} does not apply son_manipulators when exhausting Cursor's deque.

Also, {{MotorDatabase()._fix_outgoing}} and {{MotorDatabase()._fix_incoming}} methods are missing, so the fix is 2-part.

PS: As far as I understand, {{find_one}} and {{next_objext}} methods work as expected since they run on the delegate.

summary: MotorGridFS.put() example is wrong
description:
Florian Ludwig reports:

The example here:
http://motor.readthedocs.org/en/stable/api/gridfs.html?highlight=gridfs#motor.MotorGridFS.put

is a little evil since if someone really does:
{code}
    f.write(data)
    f.close()
{code}
you get corrupted files since the code does not wait for the write to finish.

This actually misled a coworker of mine.

summary: TypeError: 'MotorDatabase' object is not callable
description: When I call *motor.MotorClient().open_sync()*, it raised this. The version i install is the latest on github https://github.com/mongodb/motor

summary: Provide examples of synchronous authentication
description:
Within my project, we tried to use `MotorDatabase.authenticate` when starting up our application, and it was tricky because it’s an async method, but the Tornado IOLoop hasn’t started yet, so we can’t use yield to make it block... and we actually do want it to block.

We since discovered that we can work around this and obviate the need to use `authenticate` by passing a MongoDB URI to the MotorReplicaSetClient, and including the credentials in that URI.

But since `authenticate` is in the docs, it isn’t unreasonable to think that others will see it and try to use it, and will encounter similar issues that my team did… the option of an `authenticate_sync` method would be handy and would be consistent with the existence of `MotorReplicaSetClient.open_sync`.

summary: open_sync() interferes with Tornado's logging config
description:
Because motor start a private iolloop in open_sync, and ioloop.start may reset logging config


    ...

        if not logging.getLogger().handlers:
            ...
            logging.basicConfig()
    ...

summary: Update auth and ssl docs, examples, and tests.
inputs is a reserved keyword
description:
When I try to add a List field called "inputs" to a document, I get this error:

db.i_object.insert({inputs:[1,2,3]})
Can't extract geo keys from object, malformed geometry?:{ 0: 1.0, 1: 2.0, 2: 3.0

This should not be a reserved keyword or at least should give an useful error. Don't even understand what is this geo keys stuff doing in there.

 Made me lose the day.

summary: Test SSL and Python 3 in Jenkins
TypeError during Kerberos authentication
Should check explicitly for None for greenlet parent
description:
In a few places where Motor asserts gr.parent is available should be done as:
assert gr.parent is not None, ...

This is because the greenlet class treats dead greenlets as false-like. This can be an issue when using existing greenlet code with motor as say:
Grandparent->parent->child

If the parent dies, the greenlet code in motor should just switch to the grandparent via the built in mechanism

summary: Support Python 2 and 3 with single source
description: [2to3 turns out to be more trouble than it's worth|https://docs.python.org/3/howto/pyporting.html#writing-source-compatible-python-2-3-code]. Rewrite Motor and its tests in the common subset of Python 2.6, 2.7, and 3.2+.

summary: Remove "nose" from direct tests
description:
Improvements in the standard unittest module, in Python 2.7+, obviate the need for "nose" for Motor's basic tests and test\_motor\_ha. PyMongo is moving off nose, and so should Motor. Remove nose and rely on unittest2 in Python 2.6.

synchrotest has deeper nose dependencies, that'll be a separate ticket.

summary: Connections are not closed
description:
We tried using Motor in our production environment, which has servers running 8 Tornado processes each.
Each process does some DB queries to MongoDB, using Motor.
Each server runs a single mongos instance to which all 8 processes connect locally.

We handle around 2k requests per second, and we noticed that there has been a growing number of open connections to our MongoDB servers, over time. 

At the moment, I can't remember whether these connections were local (processes to mongos) or remote (mongos to mongo servers).

For the time being, we've implemented a thread pool solution calling pymongo synchronously, but we'd really like to use Motor, since it's native Tornado.

Is the connection "leak" a known problem? is there a way for us to force close connections? Like I said, I'm not sure if these connections are local or remote, but I guess if Motor leaves an open connection, so will mongos.

Your help is appreciated!

- Roi

summary: Motor takes 100% CPU when deleting cursor object
description:
MotorCursor and MotorCommandCursor objects implement a \_\_del\_\_ method that checks if the cursor is still alive on the server and, if so, calls self.close() to send an OP_KILLCURSORS message to the server.

Starting with Motor 0.2, the coroutine implementation of self.close() changed such that it creates a new reference to the cursor object, rescuing it from garbage collection. If the cursor also has any documents in its buffer then its "alive" property is true. This created an infinite loop: each time the cursor began to delete itself, \_\_del\_\_ revived it.

An application in this situation is still responsive, since each call to self.close() yields control to the IOLoop. However, it pegs a CPU core, and sends useless OP_KILLCURSORS messages to the server as fast as it can.

The solution is to only call close() once. If self.closed is True, short-circuit.

summary: Closing cursor does not cancel iteration with each()
description: The rarely-used each() method on MotorCursor and MotorCommandCursor is supposed to immediately cancel iteration with each(). However, it doesn't cancel until the next batch is fetched.

summary: Bad aggregation-cursor docs
description:
The example snippet for MotorCollection.aggregate with a cursor is missing a "yield" statement:

http://motor.readthedocs.org/en/stable/api/motor_collection.html#motor.MotorCollection.aggregate

Additionally, aggregation is becoming prominent enough it should be included in the tutorial and / or examples sections.

Reported here:

http://stackoverflow.com/questions/24127427/python-motor-offers-fetch-next-exception-on-aggregation

summary: TypeError using GridFSHandler with tz_aware MotorClient
description:
Reported on Google Groups:

https://groups.google.com/d/msg/mongodb-user/Ructz1Z9UQQ/nK70YMojkQYJ

GridFSHandler is a static file server that retrieves file contents from GridFS via a MotorClient.

When the client sends the If-Modified-Since header, GridFSHandler parses it into a timezone-naive Python datetime and compares it to the file's upload_date from GridFS. If the latter is timezone-aware, because the MotorClient is timezone-aware, then datetime throws "TypeError: can't compare offset-naive and offset-aware datetimes".

summary: Socket leak in copy_database
description:
Motor leaks one socket in each call to copy_database.

The socket is closed by garbage collection, but it continues to count toward max_pool_size. If max_pool_size is finite (the default is 100) then the client hangs after max_pool_size calls to copy_database.

Applies to MotorClient and MotorReplicaSetClient.

Motor users apparently don't call copy_database much, or this would have been reported earlier.

summary: Memory leak if exception is raised in cursor's each() callback
description:
Leak can be reproduced in following example:
{noformat}
#!/usr/bin/env python2
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

from tornado.ioloop import IOLoop, PeriodicCallback
import motor

loop = IOLoop.instance()

def each(result, error):
	raise Exception()

def test_callback():
	con = motor.MotorClient("mongodb://admin:password@127.0.0.1:27017/admin")
	cursor = con.test.test.find({})
	cursor.each(callback=each)


clbk = PeriodicCallback(test_callback, 1000)
clbk.start()


loop.start()
{noformat}

No exception shows, does not reproduce with other tornado functions that support callbacks.

summary: Remove "copy_database" helper method.
description:
Probably not used, and it gets more and more difficult to support.

"copy_database" without auth doesn't need a helper method, MotorDatabase's "command" method is sufficient. So that's one argument for dropping the helper method.

"copy_database" with auth, however, requires special socket handling. This requirement for special socket handling complicates Motor's plan to support multiple async frameworks (MOTOR-40, MOTOR-41). Additionally, the server has added authentication features to copydb (SERVER-15684) which makes it harder to fully support from the driver.

All evidence indicates that people don't use copy_database from drivers. If they need it, they can use PyMongo; PyMongo 2.8 will have a full copy_database implementation.

summary: Can't force Motor to send w=1 write concern
Socket-counter leak
description:
MotorPool.get_socket() proactively checks a socket for errors if it hasn't been used in more than a second. It calls select() on the socket's file descriptor to see if socket has been shutdown at the OS level.

If this check fails, MotorPool fails to decrement its socket counter, so the closed socket is forever counted against max_pool_size.

This is the equivalent of a semaphore leak in a normal multi-threaded connection pool.

summary: Add "How to Ask for Help" section to Github readme
Allow count to work with query hints
Helpers and internal command calls that don't obey read preference shouldn't raise UserWarning
description:
Motor's analogy to PYTHON-691.

Assuming Motor 0.3 wraps PyMongo 2.7.1 or later, then most of the work to fix this bug will be done in PyMongo. A few places that Motor calls commands within internal helpers, like copy_db, may need to be addressed. 

Additionally, Motor should make similar unittest fixes as PyMongo is doing.

summary: max_pool_size not enforced
description:
If many greenlets call MotorPool.get_socket at once, the pool can expand beyond its max_pool_size (100 by default). The bug was introduced in Motor 0.2 when I enabled async DNS resolution:

https://github.com/mongodb/motor/commit/d2d811fb

create_connection() increments its counter before attempting to connect. It had been in a critical section until then. Unfortunately, once resolve() became a yielding operation, the greenlet could be interrupted when it called resolve() and the one critical section was split into two.

One after another, greenlets see that there is room in the pool, then they reach the resolve() call and yield. Once they all resume after the yield there are more greenlets than max_pool_size but they don't check again if there is room.

The impact is low because extra sockets above max_pool_size are closed as soon as they're returned to the pool, so the pool does not remain over-sized forever. This is why tests like test_max_size passed even after the bug was introduced.

The solution is to increment the pool counter before the first yield at resolve().

summary: User-facing Network Compression Drivers Documentation
description: This ticket was split from {{DRIVERS-2693}}, please see that ticket for a detailed description.

summary: connectTimeoutMS not honored for replica set connections
description:
The value appears to be hard-coded to 1000ms.

https://github.com/mongodb/node-mongodb-native/blob/3709204430a3783c41077f6c58c1b11bb7fc5053/lib/mongodb/connection/repl_set.js

This causes problems when trying to connect during a warmup phase on PaaS services such as Heroku.

https://github.com/jcottr/temps-mort

summary: Improved diagnostics for failed replica set connections
description:
When a replica set connection fails the error message can be fairly opaque, requiring lucky guesswork or heroic efforts to fix. A common problem we have been running into is connection timeouts from Heroku as shown here:

https://github.com/jcottr/temps-mort

It would be nice to have better tools for diagnosing the issue in these situations. Either better error messages (in this case, it would have been helpful to know that we were getting timeouts) or maybe a diagnostic mode you could put the driver in where it would log diagnostic messages including the individual errors it gets on connection attempts.

summary: self is undefined after connect than reconnect
Support github contributing guidelines
Callback to db.command receives error message in the wrong callback parameter
description:
When I call db.command{'convertToCapped'…} on a collection that doesn't exist, I get an error output (which is fine), but the error is returned in the result. Err is null. 

Gist is here: https://gist.github.com/4027649

summary: Document arguments to callback functions
description:
The functions:
http://mongodb.github.com/node-mongodb-native/api-generated/collection.html#count
and
http://mongodb.github.com/node-mongodb-native/api-generated/collection.html#insert
do not document the arguments to the callback function.  For insert the examples don't use the second argument so it's not clear what it is for.

An example where the arguments are documented:
http://mongodb.github.com/node-mongodb-native/api-generated/cursor.html#count

summary: Db() logger option is not documented
description:
Server() supports a logger method, which is documented here: http://mongodb.github.com/node-mongodb-native/api-generated/server.html

According to the [0.9.6-20 2011-10-04 changelog entry|http://mongodb.github.com/node-mongodb-native/changelog/changelog.html?highlight=logger#id46], Db() also supports this option, but it isn't documented here: http://mongodb.github.com/node-mongodb-native/api-generated/db.html

are missing from the online documentation.
